questions.text: Walk me through the NTFS artifacts you would examine when investigating suspected data staging and exfiltration from a Windows endpoint.

junior: would look at recently accessed files
might check the Recycle Bin
would look at file timestamps
knows to look at Documents or Desktop folders
mid: examines $MFT for file creation/modification/access/entry-modified timestamps (MACB)
checks $UsnJrnl ($Extend\$UsnJrnl:$J) for file operations journal even after file deletion
reviews LNK files at C:\Users\%user%\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\ for recently accessed files
examines Prefetch files at C:\Windows\Prefetch\ for archival tool execution (7z.exe, WinRAR.exe, robocopy.exe)
checks Jump Lists (AutomaticDestinations and CustomDestinations under AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\AutomaticDestinations\) for recent file interactions
senior: knows NTFS MACB timestamps have two sets: $STANDARD_INFORMATION (modified by SetFileTime) and $FILE_NAME (only modified by the NTFS kernel driver)
recognizes that $SI timestamp predating $FN Born timestamp is physically impossible - strong indicator of timestomping
examines shellbags (NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\BagMRU) for folder navigation history including to deleted paths and network shares
parses AmCache.hve (C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\Amcache.hve) for execution evidence of tools no longer present on disk
uses $LogFile (NTFS transaction log) for file system operation history independent of event logs
checks thumbcache.db (C:\Users\%user%\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer\) for evidence of viewed image files that may have been deleted
staff: designs forensic collection triage packages that capture all above artifact classes automatically on initial response
discusses $UsnJrnl size tuning as a detection engineering decision (default 32MB wraps frequently on active systems)
identifies coverage gaps: cloud sync clients (OneDrive, Dropbox) may not generate local LNK files for remotely staged data
maps NTFS artifact coverage to MITRE ATT&CK T1005 and T1074.001 sub-techniques to identify collection blind spots

follow_ups.probe: The $UsnJrnl has been cleared by the attacker. What other NTFS artifacts might still tell you which files were accessed or copied?
You see WinRAR.exe in Prefetch with a run count of 12 and last execution time of 3:17 AM. What other fields in the Prefetch file help you reconstruct the staging activity?
The attacker navigated to a sensitive network share to identify files before copying them but didn't open the files directly. What artifact might still record that navigation?

follow_ups.escalate: The MACB timestamps on the suspected staging archive are inconsistent: $STANDARD_INFORMATION shows creation 6 months ago but $FILE_NAME shows creation 3 days ago. What does this tell you, and how do you determine the true creation time?

follow_ups.simplify: What's the first artifact you'd look at to determine whether a specific executable was run on this machine even if the file itself has been deleted?

questions.interviewer_notes: This question separates candidates by NTFS artifact depth. Junior candidates focus on visible files; strong mid-level candidates know $MFT, $UsnJrnl, shellbags, and AmCache. The $SI vs $FN timestamp distinction is a senior-level differentiator and key anti-forensics detection technique. Watch for candidates who know Prefetch encodes last 8 execution times (not just last run) and run count.

questions.text: Describe how you would conduct a memory forensics investigation on a suspected compromised Windows host using Volatility. What plugins would you run and in what order?

junior: knows Volatility is a memory forensics tool
would look for suspicious processes
knows you need a memory dump first
mid: Volatility 3 auto-detects OS profile; Vol 2 requires --profile flag (e.g., --profile=Win10x64_19041)
runs windows.pslist and windows.pstree to see process hierarchy and identify orphaned or anomalous processes
runs windows.cmdline to see full command-line arguments for each process
runs windows.dlllist to see loaded DLLs per process and identify unusual loading paths
runs windows.netscan to identify active and recently closed network connections with associated PIDs
senior: compares windows.pslist (walks EPROCESS linked list) against windows.psscan (scans physical memory for EPROCESS structures) to find DKOM-hidden processes
uses windows.malfind to identify executable memory regions not backed by mapped disk files - indicates injected code or unpacked payloads
examines windows.vadinfo for VAD (Virtual Address Descriptor) tree to find memory regions with PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE permissions (anomalous - indicates self-modifying or injected code)
uses windows.dumpfiles to extract files including DLLs and executables from memory for offline static/dynamic analysis
cross-references network connections from netscan with process PIDs and compares against expected network behavior for each process
staff: discusses memory acquisition methods and trade-offs: WinPMEM (kernel driver), FTK Imager (crash dump), hibernation file (hiberfil.sys as offline alternative) vs. live acquisition with tool artifacts
identifies cases where memory forensics is the ONLY evidence source: fileless malware, encrypted in-memory implants, credential material in LSASS
designs memory forensics into IR workflow as a standard first-response step before any system modification or reboot
evaluates Volatility 3 vs. commercial tools (Magnet AXIOM, Belkasoft RAM Capturer) trade-offs in time-sensitive IR scenarios

follow_ups.probe: malfind flagged 22 memory regions across multiple processes. How do you prioritize which to investigate first, and what are the most common false positives that generate noise?
psscan shows a process at a specific memory offset that does not appear in pslist. What technique causes this discrepancy and what does it imply about attacker sophistication?

You've identified a suspicious process. What Volatility command shows you whether that process was communicating with external hosts, and what limitation does this have for connections that already closed?

follow_ups.escalate: You're investigating a fully fileless implant: all malicious code is in memory only via PowerShell reflection, with no disk artifacts. Walk me through exactly which Volatility plugins you'd use to reconstruct execution and extract the payload.

follow_ups.simplify: Before running any Volatility plugins, what do you need to determine about the memory image first, and how do you determine it?

questions.interviewer_notes: Memory forensics fluency is a strong signal of hands-on IR/forensics experience. Key differentiators: pslist vs. psscan distinction (DKOM-based process hiding), understanding malfind has significant false positives from JIT compilers and .NET CLR (look for PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE + MZ header), and knowing Volatility 3 plugin naming convention (windows.* prefix). Fileless malware investigation is a strong escalation that tests true depth.

questions.text: You receive a PCAP from a network sensor covering the 2-hour window of a suspected intrusion. How do you approach analyzing it to identify malicious activity and reconstruct attacker behavior?

junior: would open it in Wireshark
would look for suspicious IP addresses
would check VirusTotal for IPs and domains
would look for unusual ports
mid: starts with protocol statistics in Wireshark (Statistics > Protocol Hierarchy, Statistics > Conversations) to understand traffic composition before diving into packets
filters for DNS queries to identify C2 domains via patterns: high-entropy domain names (DGA), DNS queries with very low TTL, queries to newly registered domains
extracts HTTP/HTTPS metadata: user agent strings, host headers, URI patterns, response codes
looks for beaconing patterns: periodic connections with consistent intervals (e.g., every 60 seconds) and consistent payload sizes
uses NetworkMiner or Wireshark file export to extract files transferred over the network for hash/AV check
senior: calculates beacon interval consistency: true C2 beacons have low standard deviation in inter-packet timing; legitimate update traffic has irregular timing
analyzes encrypted TLS traffic using JA3 fingerprint (MD5 hash of TLS ClientHello fields) and JA3S (ServerHello) to identify malicious TLS client implementations without decryption
examines DNS traffic for tunneling indicators: high-entropy subdomains (>3.5 bits/character), TXT record abuse, unusually large DNS responses (>512 bytes), long base64-like subdomain strings
uses Zeek/Bro to generate conn.log, dns.log, http.log, ssl.log, files.log for structured analysis at scale rather than packet-by-packet inspection
identifies data exfiltration via volume analysis: large sustained outbound transfers to unusual destinations, base64-encoded data in HTTP POST bodies
staff: designs network forensics collection architecture: span ports vs. network taps, full packet capture vs. flow-only, retention policy for legal/compliance
addresses encrypted traffic analysis gaps and legal/privacy constraints on full packet capture in regulated environments
evaluates PCAP analysis at scale: Moloch/Arkime for packet indexing and search, Zeek + ELK for metadata analytics
builds network forensics capability into IR process: sensor placement map, data availability SLAs, automated PCAP capture triggers

follow_ups.probe: How would you mathematically differentiate a C2 beacon from legitimate software that checks for updates? What statistical properties would you measure?

You found a JA3 fingerprint matching known Cobalt Strike default configurations. The attacker is using malleable C2 with a custom profile. How reliable is JA3 attribution in this case, and what can you do if JA3 evasion is suspected?
HTTP and HTTPS traffic all looks legitimate or is encrypted. What other protocol in the PCAP could the attacker be using for C2 or data exfiltration that you haven't investigated?

follow_ups.escalate: All C2 traffic is HTTPS with a valid CA-signed certificate, traffic patterns mimic legitimate CDN traffic, and JA3 matches common browsers. What network-level analysis options remain available to you without decryption?

follow_ups.simplify: You have a 50GB PCAP with millions of packets. What's the most efficient first step to identify the most suspicious conversations without reading every packet?

questions.interviewer_notes: Network forensics depth reveals quickly. Junior candidates only know Wireshark filters. Strong mid-level candidates know beaconing analysis and file extraction. Senior differentiators: JA3/JA3S for TLS fingerprinting without decryption, Zeek log analysis for scale, DNS tunneling detection by entropy analysis. The encrypted-traffic-only scenario tests depth of understanding of what metadata remains available.

questions.text: How do you build a forensic timeline when you have evidence from multiple artifact types across multiple systems with inconsistent timestamps?

junior: would manually compare timestamps from different sources
knows you need to look at when events happened
might use Excel to organize events
mid: uses log2timeline/Plaso to ingest multiple artifact types into a single normalized timeline (NTFS artifacts, Windows event logs, Prefetch, LNK files, registry, browser history, $MFT, $UsnJrnl all in one pass)
normalizes all timestamps to UTC before cross-source correlation to avoid timezone-induced errors
uses Timesketch for collaborative timeline analysis: search filtering, tagging, and annotation at scale
identifies pivot events: confirmed malicious actions (malware execution, lateral movement) to build chronological context around
separates artifacts by reliability: $FN timestamps are more reliable than $SI timestamps for true creation time
senior: fully understands MACB: M=Last Modified ($DATA content), A=Last Accessed ($SI), C=MFT Entry Changed ($SI), B=Born/Created ($SI) and which file operations update each field
accounts for timestamp manipulation: cross-compares $STANDARD_INFORMATION vs $FILE_NAME timestamps for discrepancies indicating timestomping
cross-correlates host-based Plaso timeline with network flow data (Zeek conn.log, NetFlow) and external log sources (proxy, email gateway) for complete picture
handles clock skew across distributed systems: checks NTP configuration, uses relative event ordering when absolute times are unreliable
knows Prefetch $SI Modified = last execution time, $SI Born = when Prefetch file was first created (first execution time for Win 7+)
staff: designs multi-source timeline collection as a standard component of the enterprise forensic triage package
addresses large-scale timeline challenges: Plaso generating 10M+ events requires filtering strategy and Timesketch for collaborative triage
evaluates Elastic Security vs. Timesketch for different organizational contexts and evidence volumes
designs investigation workflow: build super-timeline first before examining individual artifacts to avoid anchoring bias

follow_ups.probe: Plaso generated 4 million events from a single endpoint. How do you reduce noise and focus analysis on events relevant to a specific 4-hour intrusion window?
Windows event logs are stored in UTC. Your collected $MFT timestamps appear to be in local time (EST). How do you verify which is which, and what's the consequence of getting this wrong?
Your host-based timeline has gaps during the most critical 30 minutes of the intrusion. What external evidence sources would you add to fill those gaps, and what specifically can each contribute?

follow_ups.escalate: Your timeline shows a 6-hour gap with zero log activity on the target system. The event logs and $UsnJrnl are both clean during that period but the system was powered on. What are your hypotheses and how do you investigate each?

follow_ups.simplify: What open-source tool would you use to take NTFS artifacts, Windows event logs, and browser history from a single system and merge them into a single chronological timeline?

questions.interviewer_notes: Timeline analysis is the backbone of forensic investigation. Knowing specific tools (log2timeline/Plaso, Timesketch) vs. generic answers is the first differentiator. Full MACB understanding and $SI vs $FN timestamp awareness separates mid from senior. The 6-hour gap scenario tests hypothesis generation under artifact absence - strong candidates consider Prefetch deletion, hibernation, or attacker disabling of logging.

questions.text: During an investigation you find evidence of timestomping on files the attacker placed on the system. How do you detect it, and how does it affect your investigation?

junior: knows timestomping changes file timestamps
knows attackers do it to hide files
would look at other files created at the same time
mid: compares $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps (modified by Windows API SetFileTime - attacker can change these) against $FILE_NAME timestamps (only the NTFS kernel driver modifies these at file creation/rename) - inconsistency indicates tampering
flags physically impossible timestamps: $SI Modified predating $SI Born (Created) - a file cannot be modified before it exists
notes that common tools like Metasploit's timestomp module, BulletPassView's timestomp, and PowerShell Set-ItemProperty only modify $SI timestamps, not $FN
examines $LogFile and $UsnJrnl for the actual file operation sequence to identify true activity timestamps
checks $MFT entry sequence number and MFT record number for relative ordering between files when absolute timestamps are unreliable
senior: knows that parent directory $I30 (index records within the directory's MFT entry) retain $FN timestamps of entries at the time of their addition - independent of subsequent file timestamp manipulation
parses $I30 slack space (remnants of deleted file entries) to find evidence of files that existed in a directory even after deletion

cross-correlates Prefetch file timestamps (Prefetch $SI Modified = last execution of the executable), LNK file creation times, and event log entries (EID 4688 for process creation) to establish true timeline independent of file timestamps
identifies double-timestomping: APT29/Cozy Bear is documented modifying $FN timestamps as well using kernel-mode access - much harder to detect and requires $I30 analysis
recognizes that copied files inherit $SI timestamps from source but get NEW $FN timestamps reflecting the copy operation - useful for distinguishing moved vs. timestomped
staff: designs detection rules that flag $SI/$FN timestamp inconsistencies at fleet scale using EDR telemetry or NTFS artifact parsing
articulates the forensic significance in legal proceedings: demonstrates to non-technical audience that the attacker deliberately attempted to conceal their timeline
evaluates anti-forensics sophistication level: timestomping indicates attacker awareness of forensic techniques
documents timestamp analysis methodology with sufficient rigor to withstand cross-examination in legal proceedings

follow_ups.probe: You confirmed timestomping via $SI vs $FN discrepancy. The attacker also modified $FN timestamps using a kernel driver (double-timestomping). What artifact sources remain reliable for establishing true timeline?
Exactly where in the NTFS structure is $I30 located, and why does the parent directory's index record contain reliable timestamps even after the attacker timestomped the file itself?
All file timestamps appear to have been manipulated. What artifact records the REAL time when a specific executable was run on this system, and what is its location on disk?

follow_ups.escalate: The attacker used a custom kernel driver to manipulate both $FN and $SI timestamps, cleared $LogFile and $UsnJrnl, and the directory $I30 records were also modified. The executable in question has been deleted. What evidence of when this file was executed still exists?

follow_ups.simplify: If an attacker runs a tool that changes a file's creation timestamp in Windows using the standard API, which specific part of the NTFS file metadata did they change, and what part of NTFS might they have missed?

questions.interviewer_notes: Timestomping knowledge is one of the strongest NTFS forensics differentiators. Mid-level must know $SI vs $FN comparison cold. Senior must know $I30 directory index entries, $LogFile cross-reference, and double-timestomping (APT29 documented behavior). The double-timestomping escalation is designed to find genuine experts. Prefetch timestamp cross-correlation is a key real-world recovery technique.

questions.text: You need to forensically investigate a terminated AWS EC2 instance that was used during an intrusion. How do you approach evidence collection given the ephemeral nature of cloud infrastructure?

junior: knows the instance might be gone
would look at CloudTrail logs
would try to preserve logs before the instance terminated
mid: knows EBS root volumes may persist after instance termination if 'DeleteOnTermination' is set to false (not the default)
creates a forensic EBS snapshot immediately before any other action: aws ec2 create-snapshot --volume-id vol-xxx --description 'forensic-preserve'
mounts EBS snapshot as a NEW read-only volume on a separate forensic analysis instance - never mount original
checks CloudTrail (us-east-1 is not the only region - check all regions) for EC2 API calls: RunInstances, CreateSnapshot, DescribeInstances, GetConsoleOutput
reviews VPC Flow Logs for the instance's ENI - stored in S3 or CloudWatch Logs independently of the instance lifecycle
senior: knows EC2 memory is NOT preserved after termination - this is an irreversible evidence loss requiring live acquisition if instance is still running
if instance is running: uses AWS SSM Run Command to execute memory capture (WinPMEM, LiME for Linux) before termination
checks GuardDuty findings which are preserved in CloudTrail and GuardDuty API independently of instance termination
examines per-bucket S3 access logs if the instance role accessed S3 (not enabled by default - must be configured per bucket)
reviews IMDSv1 vs IMDSv2 token usage in CloudTrail (GetMetadataToken calls): IMDSv1 absence means metadata was accessed without auth - SSRF attack vector (T1552.005)
checks CloudWatch Logs if instance had CloudWatch agent configured - OS and application logs may persist independently
staff: designs automated forensic preservation as Lambda triggered by GuardDuty finding: snapshot EBS, capture console output, export CloudTrail scope before human response begins
addresses cross-region investigation: S3 CloudTrail must have multi-region trail enabled; default trail only covers home region
designs proactive architecture: all EC2 instances ship logs to centralized CloudWatch/S3, VPC Flow Logs always enabled, CloudTrail data events for S3 enabled by default
addresses legal hold mechanisms in AWS: S3 Object Lock for evidence preservation, preventing CloudTrail log deletion

follow_ups.probe: You've created the EBS snapshot. Walk me through the exact AWS CLI commands to mount it read-only on a forensic analysis instance without contaminating the evidence.

CloudTrail shows the EC2 instance made GetMetadataToken API calls with a 0-second TTL, followed by SSRF-like behavior. What specific IMDSv1 endpoint were they likely targeting and what credentials could be retrieved?
The EC2 instance is terminated and the EBS volume was deleted on termination. What network-level evidence source still exists to reconstruct what the instance communicated with?

follow_ups.escalate: Investigation reveals the attacker used the EC2 instance's IAM role credentials to make thousands of S3 GetObject calls before the instance terminated. S3 access logging was not enabled on the target bucket. How do you determine what data was exfiltrated?

follow_ups.simplify: Before you lose any cloud forensic evidence after an incident, what's the single most important action to take immediately regarding the disk, and why?

questions.interviewer_notes: Cloud forensics is increasingly non-optional. Key differentiators: knowing EBS volumes may not survive termination (DeleteOnTermination default), understanding VPC Flow Logs are independent evidence sources stored in S3/CloudWatch, and knowing memory is irrecoverable after termination. The IMDSv1 SSRF attack vector (T1552.005) is critical. S3 data event CloudTrail not being enabled by default (major gap for S3 exfiltration investigation) is a senior-level insight.

questions.text: An employee suspected of IP theft used their personal iPhone. What forensic artifacts can you obtain through different acquisition methods, and what are the key limitations?

junior: would look at what apps are installed
would check if they connected to the work Wi-Fi
knows you need the passcode to access it
mid: logical extraction via iTunes/Finder backup: obtains most app databases (SMS sms.db at /private/var/mobile/Library/SMS/, call history at CallHistory.storedata, contacts, calendar, notes)
backup encryption: unencrypted backup misses keychain items (passwords, certificates); encrypted backup with known password recovers keychain
filesystem extraction requires exploit (checkm8 vulnerability works on A5-A11 chips: iPhone 4s through iPhone X)
examines iOS backup artifact paths: SMS/iMessage (sms.db), Photos (Photos.sqlite), Location (CellularUsage.db), browser history (History.db under Safari/)
checks MDM enrollment for corporate devices: installed apps, compliance status, remote wipe capability
senior: knows GrayKey (Grayshift) and Cellebrite Premium for BFU (Before First Unlock) and AFU (After First Unlock) extraction on recent iOS versions - success rate varies by iOS version and chip
identifies encrypted vs unencrypted filesystem partitions: keychain data class A (complete protection) only available AFU; class D accessible BFU

examines KnowledgeC.db at /private/var/mobile/Library/CoreDuet/Knowledge/knowledgeC.db: records app foreground/background events, device lock/unlock, user interactions with precise timestamps - key usage timeline
checks biome files at /private/var/mobile/Library/Biome/ for detailed behavioral and usage data preserved even after app deletion
iCloud backups require separate legal process (18 U.S.C. § 2703 d order or warrant to Apple) and have different content than local backups
location forensics: /private/var/mobile/Library/Caches/locationd/clients.plist and consolidated.db (iOS 4-7) or significant_locations.db for movement history
staff: designs mobile forensics process with legal authorization framework: search warrant specifics for device vs. iCloud vs. carrier records
knows MDM remote wipe risk: device must be placed in Faraday bag immediately upon seizure to prevent remote wipe command before acquisition
evaluates Cellebrite UFED vs GrayKey vs open-source (iLEAPP, libimobiledevice) based on iOS version, chip, and case type
addresses chain of custody for mobile: PIN attempt logging, documentation of unlock attempts, acquisition methodology for court admissibility

follow_ups.probe: The employee's device is an iPhone 13 with an A15 chip. Why won't checkm8 work, and what are your options for filesystem-level extraction?
KnowledgeC.db shows the suspect used a specific app at 11:47 PM for 23 minutes. The app and all its data have since been deleted. What does this record prove, and what does it NOT prove?
The suspect had iCloud backup enabled and syncing enabled for Photos and iMessage. What can you get from iCloud that you can't get from the physical device, and what legal process is required?

follow_ups.escalate: The suspect remotely wiped the iPhone 15 minutes after you notified HR about the investigation. The device is now showing the setup screen. What evidence sources outside the wiped device still exist, and what can each prove about the exfiltration?

follow_ups.simplify: If you can only do a logical extraction via iTunes backup without jailbreaking, what are the three most valuable artifacts you recover, and what forensic questions can each answer?

questions.interviewer_notes: Mobile forensics is a wide variability domain. Key differentiators: knowing checkm8 only works on A5-A11 chips (not A12+/current devices), knowing KnowledgeC.db as the forensic gold mine for app usage timeline, and understanding MDM remote wipe as a critical risk during seizure. iCloud legal process (18 U.S.C. § 2703) and the distinction between AFU and BFU extraction separates senior candidates.

questions.text: What file system, registry, and behavioral artifacts does Windows malware typically leave behind, and how would you systematically hunt for these across an enterprise?

junior: would look for suspicious files in System32 or Temp
would check the Startup folder
would run a virus scanner
knows malware creates files
mid: registry Run keys: HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, HKCU same path, HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce
services: HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services (EID 7045 for new service installation)
scheduled tasks: XML files at C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\ (EID 4698 for task creation, 4702 for modification)
file system drop locations: %TEMP% (C:\Users\%user%\AppData\Local\Temp\), %APPDATA% (C:\Users\%user%\AppData\Roaming\), %ProgramData% (C:\ProgramData\)
uses Sysinternals Autoruns with VirusTotal integration for comprehensive persistence enumeration on a single host
senior: WMI persistence: EventFilter + EventConsumer + FilterToConsumerBinding (query with: Get-WMIObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventConsumer) - fileless, invisible to most AV
COM object hijacking: HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\ keys override HKLM system COM registrations without admin rights - malware DLL loaded when any process invokes the COM object

Image File Execution Options (IFEO): HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\[exe.exe]\Debugger key - executes attacker binary whenever victim binary is launched
AppInit_DLLs: HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs loads DLLs into every process that loads user32.dll
DLL search order hijacking: placing malicious DLL in application directory to load before the legitimate system DLL (check for DLLs outside System32 with same names as known system DLLs)
staff: designs enterprise-scale hunting: PowerShell remoting + Autoruns in CSV mode across all endpoints to collect persistence data centrally
develops YARA rules from malware behavioral artifacts (specific mutex names, registry key patterns, file naming conventions) for proactive hunting
maps all identified persistence locations to MITRE ATT&CK T1547 sub-techniques (001 through 015) for systematic coverage analysis
builds detection rules in EDR/SIEM for each persistence mechanism with appropriate fidelity thresholds to minimize false positives

follow_ups.probe: You find a WMI EventConsumer subscription. Walk me through the PowerShell commands to enumerate what event triggers it, what it executes, and how to disable it without destroying evidence.

A scheduled task was created at 3:17 AM. The XML file looks normal but executes a PowerShell command. What specific fields in both the XML artifact and Event ID 4698 tell you this is malicious rather than a legitimate system task?
The malware has no Run keys, no scheduled tasks, no services installed. It survives reboots. Where else might it maintain persistence that Autoruns might not show by default?

follow_ups.escalate: Hunting reveals COM object hijacking via HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID on 200 endpoints. The malicious DLL is signed with a certificate from a legitimate but compromised software vendor. How do you verify certificate legitimacy and trace the supply chain compromise?

follow_ups.simplify: Beyond the registry Run keys and Startup folder, name two other common Windows persistence mechanisms that malware uses and where you'd find evidence of them.

questions.interviewer_notes: Malware persistence knowledge separates analysts with hands-on hunting experience from those with only theory. WMI persistence is a critical senior differentiator: it's fileless, persists across reboots, is invisible to most AV, and widely used by APTs including APT29 and Lazarus Group. COM hijacking and IFEO are advanced techniques revealing deep Windows internals knowledge. Autoruns knowledge is table stakes for mid-level.

questions.text: Walk me through how you would investigate encrypted HTTPS traffic that you cannot decrypt. What can you extract without the private key?

junior: would try to look at unencrypted traffic
knows HTTPS is hard to analyze
might check DNS queries for the domains
mid: analyzes TLS handshake metadata (all unencrypted): TLS version, cipher suites, ClientHello extensions, session ticket presence
computes JA3 fingerprint from ClientHello: MD5(SSLVersion,Ciphers,Extensions,EllipticCurves,EllipticCurvePointFormats) - identifies specific TLS client implementations including known-malicious tools
computes JA3S fingerprint from ServerHello to identify specific server implementations
examines TLS certificate metadata: Subject/Issuer CN, SAN fields, serial number, validity period, self-signed vs CA-issued (self-signed C2 is a common indicator)
checks DNS queries preceding TLS connections for domain reputation and resolution patterns
senior: knows SSLKEYLOGFILE environment variable captures NSS/OpenSSL TLS pre-master secrets to a file for post-hoc decryption in Wireshark without the server private key - works for browsers and any app using OpenSSL/NSS
identifies domain fronting indicators: DNS/SNI show legitimate CDN domain but actual Host header routes to C2 - detectable by comparing SNI in ClientHello against DNS query patterns
uses Zeek ssl.log to extract JA3, JA3S, certificate chain, cipher, validation status for ALL TLS connections at scale with one line per connection
performs traffic volume and timing statistical analysis: beacon interval consistency (standard deviation of inter-connection times), payload size distribution, bytes per connection patterns
identifies DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) usage to bypass DNS monitoring: traffic to 8.8.8.8:443 or 1.1.1.1:443 with DoH SNI as C2 bypass technique
staff: designs TLS inspection architecture: where to decrypt (network perimeter vs. endpoint), certificate pinning bypass detection, performance impact assessment
evaluates metadata-only approach vs. full decryption based on threat model and legal/privacy constraints
addresses QUIC/HTTP3 forensic challenges: QUIC encrypts more of the transport layer, reducing available metadata vs TCP/TLS
designs detection strategy for encrypted C2 that accepts some limitations: behavioral analytics on metadata when content unavailable

follow_ups.probe: Two different malware families share the same JA3 fingerprint as Chrome 108. How does this affect your investigation, and what additional signals help you distinguish attacker traffic from legitimate browser traffic?

How would you capture TLS session keys from a suspicious non-browser process that you believe is using OpenSSL? What would need to be true about the process runtime environment for this technique to work?
You can't decrypt the traffic. What specific TLS certificate metadata - available without decryption - would raise suspicion about a C2 connection?

follow_ups.escalate: The malware uses domain fronting over HTTPS: SNI shows cloudfront.net, DNS resolves to a legitimate CDN IP, but the encrypted Host header routes to the actual C2 infrastructure. What network-level evidence allows you to detect this without decrypting the connection?

follow_ups.simplify: Even without decrypting HTTPS traffic, what 3 pieces of information are visible in the TLS handshake that can help you identify a suspicious connection?

questions.interviewer_notes: Encrypted traffic analysis is a critical modern forensics skill. JA3/JA3S knowledge is a strong mid/senior differentiator. SSLKEYLOGFILE technique is known primarily by practitioners who've actually needed to debug encrypted malware traffic. Domain fronting detection without decryption (comparing SNI to Host header patterns at scale) tests the deepest level of understanding. Good candidates know metadata analysis is still highly valuable.

questions.text: How do you detect process injection in a live Windows system and in a memory dump? Walk me through specific indicators for at least 3 injection techniques.

junior: knows process injection puts malicious code into another process
would look for suspicious processes
might check for unsigned DLLs
mid: classic DLL injection: evidence is a DLL loaded from unexpected path (AppData, Temp) in a target process (dlllist in Volatility)
process hollowing: legitimate process with suspicious memory regions - Volatility malfind shows MZ/PE header in memory not backed by disk file, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE permissions
checks for anomalous parent-child relationships: svchost.exe spawned by anything other than services.exe is suspicious
uses Volatility cmdline to find processes with command-line arguments that don't match the supposed process function
live detection: Sysinternals Process Explorer shows DLLs with no file path (memory-only) or DLLs loaded from unexpected directories
senior: reflective DLL injection: no disk artifact (DLL never written to disk), DLL loaded via custom loader in host process memory - Volatility dlllist shows DLL with no disk-backed path, malfind identifies the region

process doppelganging (T1055.013): uses Windows NTFS transactions to load executable from a deleted file - creates file in transaction, maps section, rollback transaction (file disappears) but the mapping stays live; malfind may miss it because memory IS backed by a file section even though the file no longer exists on disk
thread injection (T1055.003): CreateRemoteThread in target process with start address in anomalous memory region - Volatility: windows.threads plugin shows thread start address outside any known DLL

identifies userland API hooking: comparing IAT (Import Address Table) entries in process memory against the clean on-disk PE to find redirected function pointers (common AV/EDR technique also used by malware)

examines VAD (Virtual Address Descriptor) tree via Volatility vadinfo for memory regions marked VadS (private, not mapped from a file) with executable permissions - classic indicator of injected shellcode
staff: designs EDR detection rules for injection API sequences: NtWriteVirtualMemory + NtCreateRemoteThread events in same process context as suspicious target
evaluates modern evasion techniques: Early Bird APC injection (T1055.004), process ghosting - and EDR coverage gaps for each
develops YARA rules targeting PE characteristics in memory (MZ magic bytes, PE header structure) for fleet-wide hunting of injected payloads
documents process injection findings with full API call chain reconstruction for malware reverse engineering team handoff

follow_ups.probe: malfind shows a region in svchost.exe with MZ/PE header and PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE permissions. Walk me through exactly what Volatility commands you run next to extract and analyze the injected payload.

Reflective DLL injection leaves no disk artifact and the DLL doesn't appear in the normal loaded module list. What specific memory characteristics in Volatility output distinguish reflective DLL injection from a normal DLL loaded by the OS?
You've identified code injection occurred in explorer.exe. How do you determine which specific memory region contains the injected code and where the injected thread is executing?

follow_ups.escalate: The attacker used process doppelganging: the injected code executes but the file that backed the section no longer exists on disk. Standard malfind doesn't flag it because the memory IS file-backed. How do you detect process doppelganging specifically?

follow_ups.simplify: In a Volatility memory analysis, what single plugin and what specific output characteristics most directly indicate that malicious code has been injected into a legitimate Windows process?

questions.interviewer_notes: Process injection detection tests hands-on memory forensics depth. The malfind plugin with PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE + MZ header is the key mid-level indicator. Reflective DLL injection (no disk artifact, custom loader) and thread start address analysis separate senior from mid. Process doppelganging (abuses NTFS transactions to evade standard malfind detection) is an advanced technique known mainly by malware analysts and red teamers.

questions.text: You're investigating an intrusion where the attacker deployed sophisticated anti-forensics: event logs cleared, Prefetch disabled, $UsnJrnl zeroed, and Sysmon uninstalled. What forensic evidence remains available?

junior: would look for other logs
would check if there are backups
might check antivirus logs
mid: VSS (Volume Shadow Copies): vssadmin list shadows - may contain pre-wipe logs and artifacts if not explicitly deleted (check: vssadmin delete shadows destroys this)
event log clearing itself leaves traces: EID 1102 (Security log cleared by user) and EID 104 (System log cleared) if the attacker didn't immediately clear again
ShimCache (AppCompatCache) at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache stores execution evidence and survives log clearing
AmCache.hve at C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\Amcache.hve records SHA-1 hashes and metadata of executed programs
WER (Windows Error Reporting) files at C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\WER\ReportArchive\ persist independently of event log clearing
senior: SRUM (System Resource Utilization Monitor) at C:\Windows\System32\SRU\SRUDB.dat: 30-60 day history of application execution (NetworkDataUsageMonitor, ApplicationResourceUsageSummary tables), network usage per application per interface, push notifications - rarely targeted by malware
Windows Search Index at C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Search\Data\Applications\Windows\Windows.edb: filenames and content of indexed files, persists after file deletion

hibernation file (hiberfil.sys) and page file (pagefile.sys) may contain memory artifacts including process command lines, network buffers, and fragments of cleared event log data that was in memory at the time
Browser artifacts frequently survive: Chrome at C:\Users\%user%\AppData\Local\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\History (SQLite), places.sqlite for Firefox - malware rarely targets these
LNK files and Jump Lists created before anti-forensics may survive if attacker focused only on log clearing
external log sources: DNS resolver logs (firewall/router), DHCP server logs, proxy logs, email gateway logs, EDR cloud telemetry stored before Sysmon was uninstalled
staff: evaluates the anti-forensics sophistication: disabling Prefetch (requires registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management\EnablePrefetcher=0) and zeroing $UsnJrnl indicates high capability and forensic awareness
estimates investigation gap: what timeframe cannot be reconstructed vs. what remains provable
builds investigation from external evidence as primary source: network sensors, cloud-stored EDR telemetry, email gateway as the authoritative record when host evidence is destroyed

drives architectural lessons: centralized log forwarding (Splunk, Elastic) as the defense against host-based log clearing - if logs were forwarded before clearing, the clearing itself is the last event, not a gap

follow_ups.probe: You found 3 VSS shadow copies. How do you access the files inside them from a live system without modifying the original volume or the shadow copies?
SRUM database is intact. Which specific tables in SRUDB.dat tell you about network communications from a specific process, and what specific fields do you extract to reconstruct data exfiltration?
All host-based evidence sources have been destroyed. What off-host evidence sources do you pivot to, and what specific questions can network-based evidence answer that host artifacts cannot?

follow_ups.escalate: The attacker also deleted all VSS copies (vssadmin delete shadows /all), cleared the SRUM database, and filled the pagefile with zeros. EDR cloud telemetry was forwarded but the attacker operated for 6 hours before the EDR agent was installed. Name 5 remaining artifact sources and what each can specifically prove about attacker activity.

follow_ups.simplify: Event logs have been cleared. Name two Windows artifacts that record program execution history that attackers commonly miss when covering their tracks, and where exactly on disk you'd find each one.

questions.interviewer_notes: This question tests forensic resilience when primary evidence is destroyed. SRUM is the critical senior-level artifact most analysts underestimate - 30-60 days of app execution and network data, rarely cleared by attackers. VSS knowledge is mid-level table stakes. The complete anti-forensics escalation forces systematic enumeration. Strong candidates pivot to external evidence sources (network sensors, cloud EDR telemetry) as the key architectural insight.

questions.text: Describe your approach to static and dynamic analysis of a suspected malware binary. Walk me through the specific steps, tools, and what you're looking for at each stage.

junior: would upload to VirusTotal
would run it in a sandbox like Any.run or Hybrid Analysis
would check if AV detects it
mid: static - initial triage: file type (file command, PE header check), hash (MD5, SHA-256) against threat intel
static - strings extraction: strings -n 8 on Linux, Sysinternals strings.exe, or FLOSS for obfuscated strings
static - PE analysis: import/export table (imports indicate capabilities: WinSock = networking, CryptAcquireContext = crypto), section entropy (>7.0 suggests packing)
dynamic - safe execution: Cuckoo Sandbox, Any.run, Joe Sandbox in isolated environment, monitor process creation (EID 4688), file system changes, registry modifications, network connections
checks sandbox output for IOCs: C2 IPs/domains, dropped file hashes, mutex names
senior: uses Detect-It-Easy (DIE) or PEiD for packer identification and selects appropriate unpacking strategy (automated: UPX --decompress; manual: x64dbg OEP hunting)
uses FLOSS (FireEye FLARE Obfuscated String Solver) to automatically deobfuscate stack strings and encoded strings without full unpacking
examines imports for capability inference: VirtualAlloc + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread = likely injection; InternetOpenUrl + URLDownloadToFile = downloader
traces dynamic imports: GetProcAddress calls at runtime reveal functions the static import table deliberately hides
uses x64dbg or IDA Pro/Ghidra for initial anti-analysis bypass: identify IsDebuggerPresent/CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent calls, timing checks (GetTickCount before/after sleep), and patch/NOP them
patches sleep timers and beacon intervals to accelerate analysis of C2 communication behavior
staff: designs malware analysis lab: FLARE VM (Windows REMnux for Linux), snapshot-per-analysis workflow, isolated network with FakeNet-NG for simulated internet services
establishes sample management pipeline: MISP for IOC extraction and community sharing, VirusTotal Retrohunt for historical sample identification
integrates malware analysis findings into threat intelligence and detection engineering: extracted YARA rules, ATT&CK technique mapping, C2 infrastructure analysis
evaluates depth of analysis investment: sandbox-only vs. static+dynamic vs. full RE based on novelty, threat actor attribution value, and detection engineering need

follow_ups.probe: VirusTotal shows 3/72 detections and the binary was first seen 6 hours ago. Is this malicious? Walk me through your decision process for whether to investigate further.

The sandbox report shows no malicious activity - clean network, no persistence, no suspicious API calls. Does this mean the binary is benign? Name three specific reasons a malicious binary might appear clean in a sandbox.

The binary has very few readable strings and a minimal import table with only LoadLibrary and GetProcAddress. What does this tell you about the binary, and how do you overcome this analysis challenge?

follow_ups.escalate: The binary detects your debugging environment (IsDebuggerPresent, hardware breakpoint checks, timing attacks), exits cleanly in all sandboxes, and appears to only activate when specific environmental conditions are met (specific AD domain name in registry). How do you analyze it without those environmental conditions?

follow_ups.simplify: Before running any analysis on a PE binary, what single metric in the PE header most directly tells you if the binary is likely packed or obfuscated, and how do you measure it?

questions.interviewer_notes: Malware analysis questions reveal depth of reverse engineering skill. The sandbox-evasion follow-up is critical: sophisticated malware checks for VM artifacts (VMware registry keys, VBox drivers), specific usernames (admin, analyst, sandbox), domain membership, and number of running processes. FLOSS knowledge vs basic 'strings' command is a mid/senior differentiator. The environment-specific activation escalation tests analytical creativity.
